text:
University College Hospital

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery

Clinic Date: 17 April 2018

Dr Susan
University College Hospital
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Susan,

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT

Summary/Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy following remote traumatic brain injury; persistent cognitive difficulties; history of alcohol dependence in remission; osteoporosis; lives in supported accommodation; current smoker. Patient works in a manual, safety‑sensitive environment involving heights and intermittent machinery exposure.

Medication: Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily; Tegretol Retard (carbamazepine) 400 mg twice daily; Topiramate 75 mg morning and 100 mg evening; Vitamin B compound; Thiamine; Calcium/Vitamin D; Atorvastatin; Lansoprazole; Bisoprolol; Ramipril; Carbocisteine; Aqueous cream.

I reviewed William Harris in clinic today. He attended alone but confirmed ongoing oversight from his residential key worker. He reports feeling physically well, with improved appetite and steady weight. He has returned to light duties at work and describes tasks that sometimes involve working at height and around powered tools. He states he avoids such tasks if unwell or sleep‑deprived and that supervisors are aware of his medical history.

Seizures: He withdrew from Clobazam on 14 Jan. At that time, he had multiple seizures, one precipitated by illness. He has remained stable without seizures since. He and his workplace have maintained a log, which corroborates no events since late January. There is no history suggestive of focal warning; events previously described were generalised from onset with loss of awareness and bilateral motor activity. In view of this history and prior EEG findings, he is considered to have generalised epilepsy.

Examination: Alert and orientated, speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. Tone and power symmetrical in all four limbs; mild postural tremor likely medication‑related. Gait steady. No cerebellar signs. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable.

Investigations: Routine bloods taken in practice last month reportedly normal (U&E, LFT, FBC). DEXA previously showed osteoporosis; on supplementation. No new neuroimaging indicated today.

Plan:
- Continue Levetiracetam, Carbamazepine (Tegretol Retard), and Topiramate at current doses; no re‑introduction of Clobazam given current stability.
- Reinforce adherence, sleep hygiene, and illness management to minimise seizure risk.
- Occupational advice: given generalised epilepsy and the safety‑sensitive nature of duties (heights/machinery), he should continue to follow employer risk assessments and avoid unsupervised high‑risk tasks if unwell or sleep‑deprived; he understands to report any breakthrough events immediately.
- Check with practice when next routine bloods are due; re‑request if not done within 6 months (U&E, LFT, FBC, vitamin D as per bone health plan).
- Review in 6 months, sooner if any seizure recurrence or adverse effects.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Clinic date is 17 Apr 2018. The letter states he had multiple seizures around 14 Jan, and none since late January. Therefore, within the approximately 3-month window leading up to clinic (late Jan to mid-Apr), he has had seizures only at the start of that period and none thereafter. Because seizure-free duration is under 6 months, we report a frequency over this recent 3-month period as 'multiple per 3 month'.
